title: Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation
design_brief: A Tinder/Things-3-style review queue. iPhone 13 / Pixel 6 form factor. User reviews ~20 cards, swiping left to dismiss / right to keep. The top card must follow the finger with realistic rotation around its bottom anchor, rubber-band when pulled too far without committing, and fling off-screen with decay velocity when released past threshold. The next card scales up slightly as the top card moves. Reference: Tinder (the original), Things 3 swipe choreography, Apollo for Reddit comment swipes.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using PanResponder + Animated API instead of Gesture Handler + Reanimated 3
Linear rotation that pivots around the card center (looks fake)
Snapping back with a timing animation instead of a spring
Re-rendering React on every drag frame
Letting the underlying ScrollView intercept the horizontal pan
mobile_reasoning: On the UI thread we need 120fps tracking of the finger. Any React re-render during pan = dropped frames on a Pixel 5. The rotation pivot must be at the bottom of the card (transformOrigin via translateY before/after rotate) because that's where a real card hinges when you flick it. Velocity at release determines fling decay duration — a slow release should ease, a fast flick should leave the screen in <200ms. Web has no equivalent because there's no native fling decay primitive.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Tokens in theme.ts, component styles colocated. Card uses absolute positioning inside a relative container so cards stack.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-001, define named constants for Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-001, isolate cross_platform differences for Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-001, test Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: useSharedValue for translateX/translateY/rotateZ. Gesture.Pan().onUpdate writes shared values on UI thread. onEnd reads velocity and either withDecay (fling) or withSpring (return). Rotation derived via useDerivedValue: interpolate(translateX, [-CARD.width, CARD.width], [-15, 15]). Next-card scale derived from |translateX|.
gesture_strategy: Pan gesture with activeOffsetX([-10,10]) so vertical scroll inside the card body isn't hijacked. failOffsetY([-20,20]) cancels if user starts scrolling vertically. simultaneousWithExternalGesture not needed — top card owns the pan exclusively.
interaction_details: Light haptic on commit (Medium impact)
Card follows finger 1:1 horizontally, dampened 0.4 vertically
Rotation pivots at card bottom so it feels like flicking a real card
Past 30% width OR velocity > 800px/s → fling off with decay, else spring back
Spring back uses damping:15 stiffness:180 — overshoots once, settles in ~400ms
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-001 (Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps continuous gesture math for Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation in gesture/Reanimated worklets, commits state once at release, and tests vertical-scroll coexistence plus reduced-motion fallback.
what_makes_this_native: rn-001 is native because Swipe-to-dismiss card stack with rubber-band edges and rotation depends on RN touch semantics, platform accessibility, safe-area/keyboard behavior, and device performance constraints instead of web-only layout assumptions.

title: Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance
design_brief: A Cash-App-style 'Send Money' sheet with snap points at 25%, 60%, and 92% of screen height. The sheet must: drag between snaps with velocity-based snapping, dim the backdrop proportionally to height, dismiss by dragging below the lowest snap, contain a scrollable list that only scrolls once the sheet is fully expanded, and shrink content room when the keyboard appears (sheet stays at top snap but inner content scrolls to keep the focused input visible). Reference: Cash App, Apple Wallet, Linear Mobile share sheet.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Reaching for @gorhom/bottom-sheet without understanding what it does
Using a Modal component (wrong layer — can't blend with content behind)
KeyboardAvoidingView wrapping the sheet (fights the snap logic)
Animating backdrop opacity on JS thread
Letting the inner ScrollView consume the drag when sheet is not fully expanded
mobile_reasoning: The sheet lives inside the same React tree as the screen (so it can share context, animate alongside content, and not flash through a separate native modal window on Android). Drag-vs-scroll arbitration: when the sheet is at its top snap AND the ScrollView is at scrollY 0 AND the user drags down — the sheet should take the gesture. Otherwise the ScrollView takes it. This requires composing a Pan gesture simultaneously with the native ScrollView gesture and switching activation based on shared values. Keyboard: on iOS the keyboard pushes from bottom with a known height; on Android software keyboards resize the window. Both need different handling.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Sheet is a single component file with internal subcomponents (Handle, Backdrop). Theme tokens imported.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-002, define named constants for Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-002, isolate cross_platform differences for Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-002, test Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: useSharedValue translateY for sheet position. Backdrop opacity derived: interpolate(translateY, [maxSnap, minSnap], [0, 0.5]). On gesture end, find closest snap by combining position + velocity*0.2 — projects where the sheet would land at current velocity, then snaps there with withSpring(damping:18, stiffness:200, mass:0.8).
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pan() on the sheet header always wins. A second Pan() on the body uses simultaneousWithExternalGesture(scrollRef) and checks (snap === top && scrollY.value <= 0 && translationY > 0) to decide whether to let the sheet drag or pass through to scroll.
interaction_details: Selection haptic when snap commits
Drag follows finger with hard cap at top snap (no overscroll past 92%)
Release: projects landing point at current velocity * 0.15, snaps to nearest
Drag below lowest snap dismisses with spring + onClose callback
Inner scroll only activates at top snap when scrollY > 0
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-002 (Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Uses modal/sheet primitives for Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance; profile keyboard avoidance, snap changes, and backdrop updates while preserving modal accessibility boundaries.
what_makes_this_native: rn-002 is native because Three-snap-point bottom sheet from scratch with keyboard avoidance depends on RN touch semantics, platform accessibility, safe-area/keyboard behavior, and device performance constraints instead of web-only layout assumptions.

title: FlashList feed with image fade-in and per-row mount cost under 4ms
design_brief: An Instagram-style image feed rendering 2000+ items with mixed aspect ratios. Each row contains a header (avatar + username), a square or 4:5 image, an actions row (like/comment/share), and a caption. Must hit 60fps on a Pixel 5 while scrolling fast, images must fade in (not pop), and re-renders must be limited to the row whose like-state changed — not the whole list. Reference: Instagram feed, Threads, Cash App activity feed.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using FlatList
Re-rendering all rows when one like toggles (no React.memo with proper equality)
Inline render functions in <FlashList renderItem={...}>
Loading images at full resolution when row height knows the target size
Image fade-in via setState (causes re-render) instead of Reanimated
mobile_reasoning: FlashList recycles cells aggressively — that means each row's component instance is reused for different data. Hooks inside a row see new props but the same instance. Image cross-fade must reset when the data id changes, otherwise the old image lingers under the new one. Like-state belongs in a Map<id,boolean> in context with a useSyncExternalStore subscriber per row, so only the affected row re-renders. The estimatedItemSize must be honest — wrong estimates trigger layout passes that block the UI thread.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Theme tokens + a Row.styles file. Row component memoized.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-003, define named constants for FlashList feed with image fade-in and per-row mount cost under 4ms: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-003, isolate cross_platform differences for FlashList feed with image fade-in and per-row mount cost under 4ms: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-003, test FlashList feed with image fade-in and per-row mount cost under 4ms at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: useSharedValue opacity starting at 0. expo-image onLoad callback runs withTiming(1, {duration:220}). On data id change (useEffect on item.id), opacity resets to 0 immediately so the recycled instance doesn't show a stale image during fade.
gesture_strategy: Like button uses Gesture.Tap() for double-tap-to-like on the image (with onTouchesDown for the heart animation). Single-tap on the heart button toggles via context.
interaction_details: Double-tap image → light haptic, heart springs in (scale 0→1) then fades over 400ms
Single tap heart icon → selection haptic, toggles like state
Image fade-in over 220ms only after onLoad fires (no flash of empty cell)
Scroll fast: recycled cells reset opacity to 0 on id change so no stale image flash
Heart icon color swap is instant — only the affected row re-renders
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-003 (FlashList feed with image fade-in and per-row mount cost under 4ms): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Uses stable keys and virtualized row boundaries for FlashList feed with image fade-in and per-row mount cost under 4ms; profile scroll FPS, cell re-render count, and image/text measurement on a production-sized list.
what_makes_this_native: 1) expo-image with disk cache + recyclingKey makes the recycled-cell pattern actually work. 2) Heart animation runs entirely on UI thread via worklet — even during scroll. 3) Haptic feedback on like is a tactile signal no web app delivers.

title: Six-digit OTP input with auto-advance, paste, and shake-on-error
design_brief: A bank-app-style verification screen: six individual digit cells, large enough to read from across the room, that auto-advance as the user types, accept SMS autofill on iOS, accept clipboard paste of a 6-digit code (tap any cell, paste, all fill at once), and shake-with-haptic on wrong code. Backspace from an empty cell focuses the previous one. Reference: Robinhood verify, Cash App PIN entry, Apple Pay setup.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Six independent TextInputs each managing their own state
Polling Clipboard on focus instead of using textContentType='oneTimeCode' (iOS) / autoComplete='sms-otp' (Android)
Using Alert.alert to show 'wrong code'
Shake animation via setState toggles
Disabling paste because 'OTP shouldn't be pasted' (autofill IS paste — users need it)
mobile_reasoning: On iOS, a single TextInput with textContentType='oneTimeCode' triggers the SMS suggestion above the keyboard automatically. The cells are a visual treatment over a hidden input — actually rendering six TextInputs breaks SMS autofill. Backspace handling on Android needs onKeyPress because the value doesn't change when backspacing an already-empty input. The shake is a Reanimated sequence on translateX with a Notification (Error) haptic — both timed so the haptic hits at peak displacement, matching how real-world physical impact feels.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: OtpInput is a single component with internal styles. Tokens for cell size and active-cell glow.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-004, define named constants for Six-digit OTP input with auto-advance, paste, and shake-on-error: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-004, isolate cross_platform differences for Six-digit OTP input with auto-advance, paste, and shake-on-error: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-004, test Six-digit OTP input with auto-advance, paste, and shake-on-error at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: Active-cell border: useSharedValue width animates from 1 to 2 with withTiming(120ms). Shake on error: useSharedValue x runs withSequence(withTiming(-8, {duration:50}), withTiming(8, {duration:50}), withTiming(-6, {duration:50}), withTiming(6, {duration:50}), withTiming(0, {duration:50})). Notification haptic fires inline with first peak.
gesture_strategy: No custom gestures — the hidden TextInput receives keyboard focus normally. Tapping any cell focuses the input.
interaction_details: Selection haptic on every digit typed and on backspace
Error notification haptic synced with shake peak
Active-cell border thickens from 1px → 2px in 120ms
SMS code from messages → fills all six cells at once with one selection haptic + completion haptic
Caret pulse is a static color block (rendered only on active cell) — no opacity animation needed since the focus glow communicates 'here'
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-004 (Six-digit OTP input with auto-advance, paste, and shake-on-error): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps typing responsive for Six-digit OTP input with auto-advance, paste, and shake-on-error by validating after input boundaries, avoiding parent list re-renders, and measuring keypress-to-paint plus keyboard open/close on device.
what_makes_this_native: 1) SMS autofill suggestion above keyboard — pure iOS/Android system feature, no web equivalent. 2) Haptic-synced error shake feels like a physical 'no' from the device. 3) The visual cell row + hidden input pattern is the only way to keep autofill working while looking custom.

title: Custom shared-element transition for photo grid → detail using Expo Router
design_brief: A Pinterest/Photos-style grid where tapping an image expands it into a full-screen detail with the image animating from its grid position to its new position. The image must NOT visibly cross-fade or re-mount — the same pixels move and scale. On dismiss (drag down), the image animates back to its grid cell. Reference: Apple Photos, Pinterest, Instagram profile grid → post.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using a Modal and praying it lines up
Re-loading the image in the detail screen (will flash even with disk cache)
Using react-native-shared-element (legacy bridge approach, doesn't compose well with Expo Router)
Using stack screen presentation:'transparentModal' without owning the transition curve
Animating React state (will not hit 60fps during the swipe-down dismiss)
mobile_reasoning: Expo Router uses native stack under the hood, but for a shared-element effect we render the moving image OUTSIDE the screen hierarchy in a portal that lives above both screens. The grid cell measures its absolute window position before navigation; the detail screen measures its target frame; both go into a shared context. A Reanimated worklet interpolates a single 'progress' shared value from 0 to 1 and the floating image's transform is computed from progress and the two frames. Swipe-down dismiss reverses the same progress with velocity carrying it home. The grid cell hides its child while the transition is in flight (visibility:hidden via opacity worklet).

stylesheet_strategy.organization: SharedImageContext.tsx holds the floating image renderer and context. Grid cell + detail screen both read from context.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-005, define named constants for Custom shared-element transition for photo grid → detail using Expo Router: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-005, isolate cross_platform differences for Custom shared-element transition for photo grid → detail using Expo Router: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-005, test Custom shared-element transition for photo grid → detail using Expo Router at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: progress: useSharedValue(0). On navigate: progress.value = withTiming(1, {duration:360, easing: Easing.bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1)}). Dismiss pan: progress derived from translationY; release with velocity > 900 commits progress→0 with withTiming + runOnJS(router.back). Floating image's transform interpolates topLeft + scale from source frame to target frame using progress.
gesture_strategy: Detail screen Pan gesture, vertical-only (activeOffsetY:[-10,10]). simultaneousWithExternalGesture with inner scroll if any. failOffsetX([-30,30]) so horizontal touches inside the photo don't dismiss.
interaction_details: Tap cell → cell image fades to 0 same frame as floating image appears at source frame, animates to target frame over 360ms with custom bezier
Drag down on detail → progress reverses proportionally, background dims
Release past 120px OR velocity > 900px/s → finish dismissal, run router.back when progress reaches 0
Below threshold → spring back to fully open
No image re-load — same expo-image cache entry shared between cell and floating layer
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-005 (Custom shared-element transition for photo grid → detail using Expo Router): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Lets the native navigator own transitions for Custom shared-element transition for photo grid → detail using Expo Router; custom work stays in tab/screen options, with back-swipe, deep-link restore, and hidden-tab states profiled separately.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Shared image is the SAME pixels moving — not two images cross-fading. 2) Drag-to-dismiss with velocity carry-through is impossible in a browser. 3) Floating layer + context pattern works WITH Expo Router's native stack instead of fighting it.

title: Loading → success morph button with shape interpolation
design_brief: A Stripe/Robinhood-style submit button: starts as a 320×56 rounded rectangle reading 'Confirm payment'. On tap → text fades, button shrinks to a 56×56 circle showing a spinning ring. On success → ring completes, morphs into a checkmark, button briefly pulses green, then fades whole screen forward. On error → button shakes red, expands back to rectangle with error text. Reference: Stripe checkout, Robinhood buy confirmation, Cash App send.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Three separate button states each conditionally rendered (causes layout shift)
ActivityIndicator dropped into the button (looks generic)
Color transitions via setState
Hardcoded width that snaps instead of animating
Forgetting to disable touch during the loading state
mobile_reasoning: Width/height/borderRadius all animate together to morph rectangle → circle. Because StyleSheet doesn't support transitions, every animatable property must be a shared value applied via useAnimatedStyle. The spinner is an SVG-free Skia or Reanimated rotation on a half-arc View with overflow hidden. The success checkmark is a Skia Path drawn with a strokeDashoffset (or in our case, animating two line segments). The whole sequence must NOT block input — if the user taps during loading we ignore, but during error we accept retry immediately.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Single MorphButton component with internal Skia checkmark. Theme color tokens (primary, success, danger).
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-006, define named constants for Loading → success morph button with shape interpolation: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-006, isolate cross_platform differences for Loading → success morph button with shape interpolation: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-006, test Loading → success morph button with shape interpolation at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: width/height/radius interpolated from one progress shared value [0=idle, 1=loading, 2=success]. State transitions use withTiming(easing: Easing.bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1)). Spinner is a continuous rotate on a child View, started in loading state. Checkmark draws via two line lengths animating sequentially.
gesture_strategy: Plain Pressable with onPressIn scale-down to 0.97, onPressOut spring back. Disabled hit during loading.
interaction_details: Press: scale to 0.97 in 80ms, back to 1 in 120ms — no haptic on press (would feel chatty)
Submit: 300ms morph rectangle → circle, spinner fades in over the same window
Success: success notification haptic, green color crossfade in 240ms, checkmark draws in 360ms
Error: error notification haptic + 5-step shake (250ms total), morph back to rectangle, text reads 'Try again'
Touch disabled during loading and success; re-enabled after error settles
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-006 (Loading → success morph button with shape interpolation): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Uses UI-thread shared values and reduced-motion zero-duration paths for Loading → success morph button with shape interpolation; profile frame time during mount/exit and assert no JS callback runs on every animation frame.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Width/height/radius/color all interpolated together in one worklet pass — would require complex CSS state machine on web. 2) Notification haptic on success/error fires in lockstep with the visual peak. 3) Press-scale microinteraction is invisible but every native iOS button has it — its absence reads as 'web app'.

title: Native action sheet on iOS, Material bottom sheet menu on Android
design_brief: A 'More options' menu triggered from a header button. On iOS this must be the native UIAlertController action sheet (slides up from bottom, large blurred backdrop, destructive option in red, cancel separated). On Android this must be a Material 3 bottom sheet with leading icons per option, no separate cancel (tap scrim to dismiss). Same API for the calling code; different system convention surfaces. Reference: any first-party Apple app vs any Google app.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using one UI on both platforms (the iOS action sheet looks wrong on Android, vice versa)
Importing a 'cross-platform menu' library that splits the difference (feels like neither)
Manually building an iOS-looking sheet on iOS instead of using ActionSheetIOS
Putting destructive option AT THE BOTTOM on Android (iOS convention, not Android)
Forgetting the Cancel button on iOS (HIG requires it)
mobile_reasoning: ActionSheetIOS is a thin wrapper around UIAlertController — it gives you the system blur, the system animation curves, and the system 'feels right' for free on iOS. On Android there's no equivalent primitive; the Material design language calls for a bottom sheet with icons and no Cancel (dismissal is the scrim tap, matching how all other Android sheets dismiss). Same data shape going in, two completely different presentations coming out. Platform.OS branch is correct here — this is the textbook case for it.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: useActionMenu hook exposing show(options). Internally branches on Platform.OS. Android variant is a custom RN component (no ActionSheetAndroid primitive).
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-007, define named constants for Native action sheet on iOS, Material bottom sheet menu on Android: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-007, isolate platform_specific_behavior differences for Native action sheet on iOS, Material bottom sheet menu on Android: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-007, test Native action sheet on iOS, Material bottom sheet menu on Android at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: iOS: zero — system handles it. Android: bottom sheet uses withSpring(damping:22, stiffness:240) for entry, withTiming(180ms) for scrim. Backdrop opacity derives from translateY shared value.
gesture_strategy: iOS: none of our concern. Android: tap-outside (Pressable on scrim) dismisses. Drag-down dismiss via Pan gesture on the sheet handle.
interaction_details: iOS: system handles all animation, blur, and haptics
Android: selection haptic on tap, ripple flash on press
Android: drag handle responds to pan; release past 80px or fast flick dismisses
Android: scrim tap dismisses
Both: destructive options in red, but iOS places it separately at the top while Android inlines it with a red color treatment
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-007 (Native action sheet on iOS, Material bottom sheet menu on Android): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Branches only at platform-convention boundaries for Native action sheet on iOS, Material bottom sheet menu on Android; system UI handles native animation where possible and tests iOS/Android parity through snapshots and a11y roles.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Using the SYSTEM action sheet on iOS — pixel-identical to what Apple's own apps use. 2) Material-spec elevation, ripple, and Cancel-less dismissal on Android. 3) Same hook API hides the platform branch from feature code — but the UX absolutely differs where it should.

title: Animated circular progress ring with gradient stroke and inner number scrubbing
design_brief: An Apple Watch / Fitness app style activity ring: a thick gradient stroke arc from 0° to 360°, animating up to current value over 900ms with a deceleration curve. The center shows the percentage with a digit-roll counter that ticks up as the ring fills. The ring stroke has a soft drop shadow and the start cap and end cap are perfectly rounded. When value > 100%, the ring overshoots into a second concentric arc. Reference: Apple Fitness rings, Strava goal completion, Lifesum nutrition rings.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Trying to draw this with View borders (impossible to do a gradient stroke partial arc)
SVG with react-native-svg + Animated (slow, JS-bridge thrash)
ActivityIndicator with a transform (looks generic)
Counting up with setState every frame
Forgetting the rounded cap on the start/end of the arc
mobile_reasoning: Partial-arc gradient strokes are exactly what Skia is for — there's no other primitive that does it well on RN. Drawing happens on the UI thread when using Reanimated shared values bridged to Skia via useDerivedValue + Skia's reactive value system. The number counter must also run on UI thread (otherwise the digits and ring drift out of sync during the animation) — easiest path is to derive the displayed text from the same progress shared value using useDerivedValue + Skia's Text component.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: ActivityRing component, all Skia drawing internal. Size + colors + value as props.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-008, define named constants for Animated circular progress ring with gradient stroke and inner number scrubbing: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-008, isolate cross_platform differences for Animated circular progress ring with gradient stroke and inner number scrubbing: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-008, test Animated circular progress ring with gradient stroke and inner number scrubbing at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: progress = useSharedValue(0). On value prop change: progress.value = withTiming(target, {duration: 900, easing: Easing.bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1)}). Skia Path uses addArc with sweep angle = progress * 360. SweepGradient rotates with progress for a subtle chase effect.
gesture_strategy: None for the basic ring. Optional: pan gesture on ring scrubs progress for manual adjustment.
interaction_details: Animation fills over 900ms with deceleration — feels weighty, not snappy
Number counter ticks alongside the ring (derived from same shared value, never out of sync)
Past 100% the inner overshoot ring fills in the same red as the start
Drop shadow under the colored stroke gives depth — only the colored portion glows
Initial mount animates from 0 → value, not value snapped instantly
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-008 (Animated circular progress ring with gradient stroke and inner number scrubbing): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps Skia geometry for Animated circular progress ring with gradient stroke and inner number scrubbing memoized and updates only drawing inputs; verify GPU frame time, no hook-in-loop usage, and a text summary for non-visual users.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Gradient sweep stroke with rounded caps + soft glow — physically impossible with View borders, ugly with SVG, perfect with Skia. 2) Counter and ring stay in sync because they share one UI-thread value. 3) Overshoot ring is a real UX pattern from Apple Fitness — 'over-achievement' is communicated visually.

title: Dynamic Type-aware card layout that reflows for accessibility text sizes
design_brief: A settings row card pattern (icon + title + subtitle + chevron) that must read perfectly at the default text size but also at the largest accessibility text sizes iOS exposes (AX1 through AX5, up to ~310% scaling). Many apps either break (text clipped) or refuse to scale (locking text size — an accessibility failure). At AX sizes, the icon should move ABOVE the text and the chevron should drop to a second row, becoming a 'Open >' button. Reference: iOS Settings app at large text, Apple Mail at AX text.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Setting allowFontScaling={false} on text (locks out users who need it)
Hardcoded fontSize without using PixelRatio.getFontScale() awareness
Fixed-height rows (text gets clipped at large sizes)
Truncating to a single line at AX sizes (users can't read what they need)
Using a global lineHeight that breaks when font scales
mobile_reasoning: On iOS, PixelRatio.getFontScale() returns 1.0 at default and up to ~3.1 at AX5. On Android, the system font scale is similar but capped lower by default. Layout must NOT be height-fixed — let intrinsic height drive. At a threshold (fontScale > 1.6) we switch from horizontal row to stacked layout. Touch target stays 44pt minimum regardless. This is the bar Apple holds first-party apps to and the bar most third-party apps fail to meet.

stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-009, isolate cross_platform differences for Dynamic Type-aware card layout that reflows for accessibility text sizes: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.

gesture_strategy: Standard Pressable.
interaction_details: Pressed state darkens background slightly (no scale — would feel wrong on a list row)
On tier change (user adjusts system text size and returns to app), layout cross-fades to new arrangement
At AX sizes, the chevron becomes a labeled 'Open' button so VoiceOver users without the gesture still have a clear CTA
minHeight scales with text but never below 44pt
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-009 (Dynamic Type-aware card layout that reflows for accessibility text sizes): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Treats accessibility state as the primary contract for Dynamic Type-aware card layout that reflows for accessibility text sizes; verify TalkBack/VoiceOver output, 44px targets, action alternatives, and no extra render churn from announcements.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Listening to system font scale changes on AppState transition — this is a system-level signal that has no web equivalent. 2) Layout reflow at AX sizes is the same pattern iOS first-party apps use. 3) Respecting Dynamic Type is THE difference between an app that 'works on iOS' and an app that 'feels iOS'.

title: Lazy-mount expensive offscreen panels with InteractionManager + viewport detection
design_brief: A multi-tab analytics screen where each tab contains a heavy chart component. Initial app load must show the first tab in <300ms on a Pixel 5. Other tabs must mount lazily — first time user swipes to them, the chart is built; thereafter it's kept warm in memory. Past three tabs, oldest non-visible tab unmounts to free memory. Reference: Robinhood account tabs, Stripe Dashboard, Apple Stocks tabs.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mounting all tabs upfront 'just in case'
Unmounting and remounting on every tab switch (loses scroll position + re-renders chart from cold)
Using FlatList with horizontal pagination of heavy children
Loading chart data on initial app open before user requests it
Blocking the JS thread to construct charts (use InteractionManager.runAfterInteractions)
mobile_reasoning: Cold-mount of a heavy chart (data parsing + Skia path generation) can take 80-200ms of JS thread time on mid-tier Android. If this happens during a swipe, the swipe stutters. We solve this with: (1) lazy mount — don't construct the tree until the tab is visible, (2) InteractionManager.runAfterInteractions for the initial paint so we don't block the entering animation, (3) keep-alive — once mounted, hide via opacity:0 / pointerEvents:'none' rather than unmount, (4) LRU eviction past N tabs to bound memory. This is the pattern Robinhood / Cash App use for tab-heavy screens.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: useLazyTabs hook manages mount registry. Tab content rendered absolutely-positioned, only the active one visible.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-010, define named constants for Lazy-mount expensive offscreen panels with InteractionManager + viewport detection: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-010, isolate cross_platform differences for Lazy-mount expensive offscreen panels with InteractionManager + viewport detection: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-010, test Lazy-mount expensive offscreen panels with InteractionManager + viewport detection at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

gesture_strategy: Optional swipe between tabs via PagerView, but for this heavy-content case we restrict to tap-only on the tab bar.
interaction_details: Tab tap → indicator slides over 220ms while mounted tabs cross-fade
First-time tab tap: indicator slides immediately (visual feedback), content mounts after interactions complete (typically same frame on iOS, next frame on Android)
Inactive but mounted tabs are opacity:0 + pointerEvents:'none' — they keep scroll position and chart state
Past 3 alive tabs, oldest evicted on next switch
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-010 (Lazy-mount expensive offscreen panels with InteractionManager + viewport detection): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Defines a measurable hot path for Lazy-mount expensive offscreen panels with InteractionManager + viewport detection; compare before/after on the same device fixture and fail CI when render count, memory, or JS frame time regresses.
what_makes_this_native: 1) InteractionManager.runAfterInteractions defers work past the current gesture/animation — this is a React Native primitive that has no web analog. 2) Keep-alive pattern preserves scroll position and chart state across tab switches. 3) Bounded LRU prevents the kind of memory bloat that kills a 4GB Android device.

title: Custom camera UI with focus-and-exposure tap, pinch to zoom, swipe to switch lens
design_brief: A Halide / Cash App QR scanner / Instagram story camera UI. Full-screen preview from VisionCamera. Tap anywhere on the preview to set focus point (animated yellow square that pulses then fades). Pinch to zoom with smooth scale-based zoom curve. Swipe horizontally over the lens chip (1x / 0.5x / 3x) to switch lenses with a flicker-free transition. Capture button has the classic two-ring pulse on press. Reference: Halide, native iOS Camera app, Instagram story camera.
mobile_reasoning: VisionCamera exposes zoom as a number (the lens's native zoom factor). Human perception of zoom is logarithmic — going from 1x to 2x feels like one step, 2x to 4x feels like another step (doubling). So the gesture-to-zoom mapping must be exponential: gestureFactor ** 1.5 or similar. Focus square position lives as shared values; the square is rendered above the preview, always — never inside the camera native view. Lens switching needs the new camera initialized before the old one tears down (otherwise: black flicker).

stylesheet_strategy.organization: CameraScreen owns the preview + overlay layers. CaptureButton, FocusSquare, LensChips are subcomponents.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-011, define named constants for Custom camera UI with focus-and-exposure tap, pinch to zoom, swipe to switch lens: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-011, isolate cross_platform differences for Custom camera UI with focus-and-exposure tap, pinch to zoom, swipe to switch lens: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.

animation_strategy: Focus square: opacity + scale spring (1 → 0.85 → 1, then fade after 1.2s). Capture button: inner circle scales 1 → 0.85 on press, scales back on release. Zoom value is a shared value driven by Gesture.Pinch with exponential mapping.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Tap for focus. Gesture.Pinch for zoom — onUpdate writes zoom shared value, onEnd settles. Gesture.Pan on lens chip strip with snap-to-nearest. All three composed with Gesture.Race so only one wins at a time.
interaction_details: Tap on preview: selection haptic + yellow focus square pulses (scale 1→0.85→1) and fades after ~1.2s
Pinch zoom: exponential curve (^1.5) maps gesture to camera zoom — feels natural across zoom range
Lens chip tap: selection haptic, camera re-initializes with new lens (no black flash because zoom stays continuous)
Capture: heavy haptic + inner circle contracts then springs back
All overlays anchored above safe area
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-011 (Custom camera UI with focus-and-exposure tap, pinch to zoom, swipe to switch lens): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps picker/upload state outside render-heavy media previews for Custom camera UI with focus-and-exposure tap, pinch to zoom, swipe to switch lens; throttle progress events, preserve EXIF/privacy choices, and measure decode/upload latency on a low-memory device.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Real focus-on-tap that talks to AVCaptureDevice / Camera2 API — web cameras can't do this. 2) Exponential zoom curve matches how humans expect zoom to feel. 3) Heavy haptic on shutter — even with eyes closed you feel the photo was taken.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using the built-in expo-image-picker as a camera (no custom UI)
Linear zoom mapping that feels twitchy at 5x+
Snapping focus square to corners instead of touch point
Capture button without the classic ring contraction
Switching lenses with a black flash

title: Map with clustering markers and bottom sheet that follows map gestures
design_brief: A Yelp / Apple Maps Lookaround / Airbnb search layout: full-screen map with location pins that cluster at low zoom levels and split into individual markers at high zoom. A peeking bottom sheet shows a horizontally-scrollable card carousel of nearby results. When the user pans the map, the sheet collapses to its lowest snap. When the user taps a marker, the sheet expands to mid-snap and the selected card scrolls into view. Reference: Airbnb search, Yelp restaurant browse, Apple Maps Lookaround.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Rendering 500 markers individually (drops the map to 20fps)
Using react-native-maps PROVIDER_GOOGLE on iOS (lose Apple Maps polish)
Tightly coupling sheet position to map state via React props (cascade re-renders)
Recomputing clusters on every region change without throttling
Letting the bottom sheet capture the pan when user means to pan the map
mobile_reasoning: Clustering must happen off-thread or with a worker because 500+ marker cluster recompute on every map move is JS-thread death. The supercluster library accepts a worklet-friendly data shape. Map region change events fire frequently — debounce to 100ms. Sheet/map coordination: sheet position is a shared value; map region change reduces sheet snap via a worklet (no React render). Selected marker drives sheet to a snap AND scrolls inner FlashList — both can be done via shared value + useAnimatedReaction.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: MapScreen owns map + sheet. Cluster pin and card components separate. Sheet uses the bottom-sheet pattern from rn-002.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-012, define named constants for Map with clustering markers and bottom sheet that follows map gestures: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-012, isolate cross_platform differences for Map with clustering markers and bottom sheet that follows map gestures: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-012, test Map with clustering markers and bottom sheet that follows map gestures at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: Sheet snap driven by shared value. On region change (debounced), if not at lowest snap, animate to lowest. On marker tap, animate to middle snap and scroll FlashList to selected index.
gesture_strategy: Map owns its own gestures. Sheet pan composed as in rn-002. FlashList horizontal scroll inside sheet — paged with decelerationRate='fast'.
interaction_details: Map pan → debounced region change → sheet collapses to bottom snap
Tap cluster → mapRef.animateToRegion zooms in (no need to handle cluster expansion separately at this layer)
Tap marker → selection haptic + sheet to mid-snap + carousel scrolls to selected card
Carousel paged scrolling: cards snap with decelerationRate='fast'
Sheet drag projects landing snap via velocity * 0.15
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-012 (Map with clustering markers and bottom sheet that follows map gestures): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Bounds marker/region updates for Map with clustering markers and bottom sheet that follows map gestures; debounce location writes, cluster or window large marker sets, and measure map interaction FPS while selected-place details update.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Apple Maps on iOS via MapKit (free, no API key, beautiful) — the system map. 2) Marker tap + sheet animate + carousel scroll choreographed in one gesture response. 3) Pan-to-collapse-sheet is the Yelp/Airbnb convention users expect.

title: Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point
design_brief: A stock chart with a smooth line drawn over time. User long-presses-and-drags to reveal a vertical crosshair that snaps to the nearest data point. The price label above and the timestamp below the chart update as the user scrubs. A subtle selection haptic fires when the crosshair moves to a new data point. When the user releases, the crosshair fades out and the chart returns to showing the latest value. Reference: Robinhood, Apple Stocks, Coinbase.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using a chart library like Victory or react-native-chart-kit (slow, JS thread bound)
Setting state on every drag frame to update the price label
Computing the path on JS thread (must be Skia)
Haptics on every gesture frame (call once per data point change)
Long-press detection that fights the chart's pan
mobile_reasoning: This is a Skia + Reanimated job. The price line is a Skia Path computed once on data load. The crosshair is a Skia Line/Path driven by a shared value. The price label text is bound to a useDerivedValue that reads the shared value and looks up the nearest data point. Haptic-on-new-point: useAnimatedReaction watches the derived index and fires Haptics via runOnJS only when index changes. Long-press-then-pan is one Gesture.LongPress() followed by Gesture.Pan() composed with .simultaneousWithExternalGesture and a manual activation state.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PriceChart component, all Skia drawing internal. Header price text managed via Reanimated useDerivedValue.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-013, define named constants for Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-013, isolate cross_platform differences for Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-013, test Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: Crosshair position: shared value x. Path opacity: shared value, fades crosshair in/out. Price label: useDerivedValue returns formatted string based on x → index → data[index].price.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.LongPress(minDuration: 150) — on activation, set crosshair visible. Then a Pan can follow via Gesture.Simultaneous. On Pan end: hide crosshair.
interaction_details: Long press 150ms anywhere on chart → crosshair fades in at touch position
Pan keeps crosshair under finger (clamped to chart bounds)
Crossing into a new data point fires a selection haptic — feels like ticks
Release → crosshair fades over 200ms, price label snaps back to current
Price/time labels update on UI thread — no perceptible lag between finger and number
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-013 (Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Precomputes chart paths for Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point, keeps accessibility summaries in React text, and profiles path recalculation against dataset size and orientation changes.
what_makes_this_native: rn-013 is native because Robinhood-style line chart with scrubbing crosshair and haptic-on-data-point depends on RN touch semantics, platform accessibility, safe-area/keyboard behavior, and device performance constraints instead of web-only layout assumptions.

title: Brutalist task list with monospace type, hard shadows, and no-rounded-corners
design_brief: A productivity app screen in a brutalist aesthetic: sharp 90° corners, hard offset shadows (not blurred), heavy monospace type, only two colors plus black/white (no gradients), exposed checkboxes that look mechanical. Inspired by Are.na, early swiss design, and the Berlin school of brutalist web in mobile form. Reference: Are.na iOS app, Lichen NYC, Working Not Working.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Adding any borderRadius > 0
Using shadowRadius (blurred shadows soften brutalism — use shadowOffset only)
Adding gradients to surfaces
Sans-serif body type (must be monospace)
Adding micro-animations everywhere (brutalism is about restraint — animations should be abrupt, not eased)
mobile_reasoning: Brutalism on mobile is hard because mobile design conventions push toward soft, rounded, glanceable. Done right, it stands out — but the typography MUST be legible at mobile sizes (use a monospace cut for screens like JetBrains Mono or IBM Plex Mono, not a display monospace). Touch targets still need to be 44pt — brutalism doesn't excuse bad ergonomics. Animations should be 60ms linear, not 220ms ease-out — abrupt transitions match the visual language.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: theme.brutalist.ts owns the entire visual language. All radii are 0, all shadows are offset-only, only two accent colors.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-014, define named constants for Brutalist task list with monospace type, hard shadows, and no-rounded-corners: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-014, isolate cross_platform differences for Brutalist task list with monospace type, hard shadows, and no-rounded-corners: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-014, test Brutalist task list with monospace type, hard shadows, and no-rounded-corners at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: Check toggle: borderColor and backgroundColor swap with withTiming(60ms, easing: Easing.linear). NO spring. Pressed state: backgroundColor immediate, no fade.
gesture_strategy: Plain Pressable. Long-press deletes (linear 60ms shrink + dispatch).
interaction_details: Press: 60ms linear scale to 0.98, back to 1 — feels mechanical, not soft
Toggle: Rigid impact haptic (matches the visual hardness)
Done state inverts the card: black background, beige text — like ink stamped
No spring physics anywhere — brutalism is rigid
Checkmark is an × glyph, not a curve — typewriter-style
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-014 (Brutalist task list with monospace type, hard shadows, and no-rounded-corners): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps the visual treatment for Brutalist task list with monospace type, hard shadows, and no-rounded-corners token-driven and stable; measure layout passes after theme changes and ensure contrast/blur fallbacks do not trigger extra renders.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Rigid haptic matches the visual language — most apps default to soft haptics; choosing the harder one is taste. 2) Platform-aware shadow handling (iOS shadow vs Android offset block) gets the same visual result correctly on both. 3) The deliberate REJECTION of mobile defaults (rounded corners, blurred shadows, soft springs) is itself the design statement.

title: Swipe-to-action list rows with progressive reveal and snap-open detents
design_brief: An email-app-style swipeable list row (iOS Mail, Gmail, Things 3). Swipe left reveals two destructive actions (Archive, Delete); swipe right reveals one constructive action (Mark Read). The action background expands proportionally with the swipe — at past 60% swipe-width OR with high velocity, the row commits the primary action automatically. Tap-on-action also triggers it. Snaps to detent positions when released between thresholds. Reference: Apple Mail, Things 3, Spark Mail.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using Animated.View + PanResponder
Showing all action labels at the same size regardless of swipe distance (no progressive reveal)
Letting both directions stay open simultaneously
Snapping back without spring physics
Re-rendering the parent list on every drag pixel
mobile_reasoning: Each row owns its own gesture and shared value — the list doesn't know individual row offsets. The action background lives behind the row content; as the row translates the actions become visible. At swipe past commit threshold, the action background takes over the full row width with a color shift, signaling 'release to commit'. The auto-commit on release-past-threshold is a key iOS Mail pattern — it's faster than swipe → tap.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: SwipeRow generic component with leftActions/rightActions props. Each action defined as {label, color, icon, onPress, destructive}.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-015, define named constants for Swipe-to-action list rows with progressive reveal and snap-open detents: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-015, isolate cross_platform differences for Swipe-to-action list rows with progressive reveal and snap-open detents: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-015, test Swipe-to-action list rows with progressive reveal and snap-open detents at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: translateX shared value. On end: if past commit threshold, withTiming(-rowWidth, 200ms) then runOnJS(onCommit). Else withSpring to nearest detent (0 or -snapDetent * actionCount).
gesture_strategy: Pan with activeOffsetX([-10, 10]) so vertical scroll passes through. failOffsetY([-15, 15]) to give the list scroll priority.
interaction_details: Drag follows finger 1:1 with mild rubber-banding past 1.3x action width
Past commit threshold: selection haptic + background color shifts to commit color + label scales up
Release past commit threshold: row slides off with Heavy/Medium haptic, action fires
Release between detent and commit: spring back to nearest detent
Tap action (when revealed): same action fires immediately
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-015 (Swipe-to-action list rows with progressive reveal and snap-open detents): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps continuous gesture math for Swipe-to-action list rows with progressive reveal and snap-open detents in gesture/Reanimated worklets, commits state once at release, and tests vertical-scroll coexistence plus reduced-motion fallback.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Swipe-past-threshold auto-commits — saves a tap compared to swipe-then-tap. 2) Haptic at the commit threshold lets the user feel the commitment point without looking. 3) Action background color shift on threshold is a clear visual signal that maps to the haptic — visual + tactile reinforce each other.

title: Keyboard-aware comment composer sheet that grows from input bar
design_brief: An Instagram/Threads-style comment composer: a single-line input bar pinned to the bottom of the screen above the keyboard. When the user taps it, the bar slides up with the keyboard, and a sheet expands above it showing the comment thread. When the user starts typing a long reply, the input bar grows to multi-line (max 4 lines). Send button enables only when text is non-empty. Reference: Instagram comments, Threads replies, Apple Messages.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: KeyboardAvoidingView with behavior='padding' (causes content jump on Android)
TextInput with multiline={true} from the start (no animation on grow)
Send button always visible at full opacity even when disabled
Re-rendering the comments list when the keyboard opens
Manually subtracting keyboard height from container height (use the platform API)
mobile_reasoning: Keyboard handling on iOS vs Android is one of the most-bungled areas of RN. iOS: keyboard pushes from bottom, height known via Keyboard.addListener('keyboardWillShow'). Android: by default the window resizes via android:windowSoftInputMode='adjustResize' — but this can cause jank during the keyboard animation. The clean solution in RN 0.76+ is react-native-keyboard-controller which gives a reliable shared value for keyboard height on both platforms. The input bar's bottom inset = max(safeArea.bottom, keyboardHeight).

stylesheet_strategy.organization: CommentComposer hooks into useKeyboardHandler from react-native-keyboard-controller. Send button animation tied to text length.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-016, define named constants for Keyboard-aware comment composer sheet that grows from input bar: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-016, isolate cross_platform differences for Keyboard-aware comment composer sheet that grows from input bar: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-016, test Keyboard-aware comment composer sheet that grows from input bar at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: Input height: useSharedValue grows from onContentSizeChange. Send button: opacity + scale spring from text.length > 0. Bottom inset tracks keyboard height via useKeyboardHandler worklets.
gesture_strategy: None custom — text editing only.
interaction_details: Tap input → keyboard slides up, bar tracks keyboard height frame-for-frame (no jump)
Type → input grows from 40pt to max 120pt, then internal scroll
Text present → send button springs in (scale 0.6 → 1, fade 0 → 1)
Text empty → send button springs out
Send: selection haptic + clears input + shrinks to min height

title: Inline field validation with error shake, color shift, and announcement
design_brief: A signup form field (email + password) that validates onBlur and on submit. Invalid email: field border turns red, horizontal shake of the field row, helper text fades in below. Invalid password: same treatment plus a requirements list animates in showing which rules pass (live as user types). Reference: Stripe checkout, Notion onboarding, Linear signup.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Validating onChange (annoys the user, fights their input)
Using Alert.alert for validation errors
Color-only error state (fails accessibility)
Rule list rebuilt from scratch on every keystroke (causes jank)
Error helper text that pushes layout when it appears (use absolute or fixed reserve)
mobile_reasoning: Validation choreography: silent while typing, evaluate on blur, communicate via color + motion + text + haptic + screen reader announcement. The shake must NOT cause layout shift (it's a transform, not margin). The helper text reserves vertical space at mount so showing it doesn't push the next field down. Rule list animates per-rule with stagger — each rule is independently memoized so toggling one only re-renders that row.

Blur with invalid: red border, shake 5-step (250ms total), error notification haptic, helper text fades in
User types after error: error clears immediately, border returns to focused blue
Rule list items pop in (scale 0→1 spring) as criteria pass
No layout shift when helper text appears — slot reserved
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-017 (Inline field validation with error shake, color shift, and announcement): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.

what_makes_this_native: 1) Error haptic timed with shake peak — physical confirmation. 2) AccessibilityInfo.announceForAccessibility — screen reader users get errors at the same time as sighted users. 3) Rule list ticks live in real-time on UI thread — feels reactive in a way web forms rarely do.

title: Custom tab bar with morphing active indicator and badge counts
design_brief: A bottom tab bar (5 tabs) inspired by Instagram and Cash App. The active tab indicator is a soft-colored pill that slides between positions with a spring. Each tab can show a badge count (red dot or numeric pill). On tab press the icon does a subtle spring scale to 1.1 and back. The center tab is a special FAB-style action (compose / capture) that floats above the bar. Tab bar respects bottom safe area. Reference: Instagram, Cash App, Threads.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using the default Expo Router/React Navigation tab bar (boring, no character)
Animating indicator with Animated API
Center FAB cutout via complex SVG mask when a simple absolute-positioned button works
Hardcoded bottom padding instead of useSafeAreaInsets
Badge as text on top of icon (clips ugly on Android)
mobile_reasoning: Expo Router exposes a tabBar prop where you provide a custom component receiving the navigation state. The active indicator position is a shared value derived from the active tab index — driven by useDerivedValue with withSpring on index change. Center FAB is rendered as a sibling absolutely-positioned above the tab bar, not part of the row layout. Badge counts subscribe per-tab via useSyncExternalStore so adding a badge to tab 3 doesn't re-render tabs 1/2/4/5.

interaction_details: Tab tap: selection haptic + icon scale spring (1→1.15→1)
Indicator slides under new tab with spring (damping:22, stiffness:240) — ~350ms settle
FAB tap: medium impact haptic + navigate
Badge appears/disappears with spring scale (visible only when count > 0)
BlurView on iOS lets content underneath show through subtly
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-018 (Custom tab bar with morphing active indicator and badge counts): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Lets the native navigator own transitions for Custom tab bar with morphing active indicator and badge counts; custom work stays in tab/screen options, with back-swipe, deep-link restore, and hidden-tab states profiled separately.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Native iOS blur on tab bar — content shows through subtly, like Apple's first-party apps. 2) Spring physics on indicator + icon scale feel responsive. 3) Per-tab badge subscription means a million badge updates per second wouldn't re-render the bar.

title: Staggered list reveal on mount with entrance-exit choreography
design_brief: A settings screen or inbox that animates its rows in on first mount: each row fades up from 12px below with a 40ms stagger between rows. On data change (new items prepended), only the new items animate in. On row removal (swipe-delete), the row collapses height to 0 while fading out. Should never feel laggy — the choreography stops once items are in their final positions. Reference: Apple Mail inbox load, Linear issue list, Things 3 task list.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Animating with LayoutAnimation (no fine control, breaks on Android)
All rows animate at once (no stagger = feels cheap)
Re-running entrance animation on every re-render
Forgetting to memoize the row so it doesn't re-animate when parent state changes
Animating items already scrolled off-screen
mobile_reasoning: Reanimated 3 Layout animations (entering/exiting/layout) are the modern way. Entering: FadeInDown.delay(index * 40) — but the delay must be capped to avoid late rows appearing too long after the user expects (cap at index * 40 up to 240ms max). Exiting: FadeOutLeft + collapsing height. The animations run on UI thread via Reanimated's layout system, not the legacy LayoutAnimation which is JS-thread-bound. Mounting animation should only run once — useRef flag to prevent replay on prop change.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: AnimatedRow wraps content with layout animations. Parent component controls list mount-time gating.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-019, define named constants for Staggered list reveal on mount with entrance-exit choreography: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-019, isolate cross_platform differences for Staggered list reveal on mount with entrance-exit choreography: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
