animation_strategy: Entering: FadeInDown.duration(320).delay(min(index*40, 240)).easing(Easing.bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1)). Exiting: combine FadeOut + slidingOutLeft + custom height collapse. layout=Layout.springify() for in-list position changes when an item is removed.
gesture_strategy: None — rows are static or use SwipeRow from rn-015.
interaction_details: First mount: rows fade up + spring into position, 40ms stagger, cap at 6th row (240ms max delay)
New item prepended: animates in with delay=0 (instant entrance), other rows slide down via layout=Layout.springify
Item removed: collapses left + fades out over 220ms, remaining items spring up to fill space
Reduce Motion: all entrance/exit/layout animations skipped, items appear/disappear instantly
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-019 (Staggered list reveal on mount with entrance-exit choreography): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Uses UI-thread shared values and reduced-motion zero-duration paths for Staggered list reveal on mount with entrance-exit choreography; profile frame time during mount/exit and assert no JS callback runs on every animation frame.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Layout animations run on UI thread — at 60fps on Android mid-tier where LayoutAnimation often stutters. 2) Stagger cap prevents the 'animation tail' problem on long lists. 3) Reduced motion respected as a system signal, not an in-app preference.

title: Audio waveform player with scrub-to-seek and live progress fill
design_brief: A podcast / voice memo waveform display rendered in Skia: vertical bars representing audio amplitude (precomputed peaks data), unplayed bars in gray, played bars in the brand color. The user can tap or drag along the waveform to scrub. The current playhead is a thin vertical line that pulses subtly while playing. Reference: Overcast, Voice Memos, WhatsApp voice notes.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Drawing 200 Views as individual bars (slow, ugly on re-layout)
Animating progress with setState every 100ms
SVG path with react-native-svg (too slow for live updates at 60fps)
Linear-color-only fill (lacks polish)
No tap-to-seek — only drag
mobile_reasoning: Hundreds of bars × 60fps = Skia territory. The waveform is a single Canvas drawing each bar via a Skia Path or rect. The current play position is a shared value updated from the audio player's onProgress (throttled to ~30Hz to be polite to JS thread) — actual rendering at 60fps runs from a useFrameCallback or just on the shared value's natural update cadence. Tap-to-seek: map gesture x to (x / canvasWidth) * duration, then call audio.seekTo. Drag scrub: same plus pause-while-dragging then resume.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Waveform component all-Skia. Audio control hook separate.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-020, define named constants for Audio waveform player with scrub-to-seek and live progress fill: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-020, isolate cross_platform differences for Audio waveform player with scrub-to-seek and live progress fill: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-020, test Audio waveform player with scrub-to-seek and live progress fill at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: playProgress shared value [0..1]. Played bars derived per-frame from progress. Playhead pulse: useFrameCallback updates opacity based on Date.now() at 0.8 + sin*0.2.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Tap for seek. Gesture.Pan for scrub: onStart pauses player, onUpdate updates progress shared value AND seeks player, onEnd resumes.
interaction_details: Tap any bar: instant seek with the player resuming from that position
Drag: player pauses for scrub feel, position updates as you drag, resumes on release
Playhead pulses subtly while playing (opacity 0.7 ↔ 0.85)
Played bars fill in real-time as audio progresses
Color transition from gray to red exactly at the playhead — no antialiasing fuzz
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-020 (Audio waveform player with scrub-to-seek and live progress fill): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps Skia geometry for Audio waveform player with scrub-to-seek and live progress fill memoized and updates only drawing inputs; verify GPU frame time, no hook-in-loop usage, and a text summary for non-visual users.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Skia handles 200+ bars at 60fps where SVG would die. 2) Drag-to-scrub with pause-resume mimics how iOS Voice Memos behaves. 3) Live waveform rendering is a uniquely audio-on-mobile UI pattern with no web equivalent that doesn't feel laggy.

title: useNativeDriver-equivalent: pure-worklet derived values to avoid bridge
design_brief: A parallax product detail screen (Apple Store / Hermès style): a hero image that scales when scrolled past the top (rubber-band) AND fades + shrinks when scrolled past it. A sticky title that appears in the navigation header when the original title scrolls out of view. All animation must run entirely on UI thread — zero JS-thread work during scroll. Reference: Apple Store iOS app product page, Apple Music album view, Hermès iOS.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Listening to scroll on JS thread via onScroll setState
Using interpolate with Animated.event + useNativeDriver:false (defeats the point)
Reading layout via measure() during scroll
Re-rendering the header on every scroll frame to show/hide sticky title
Using ScrollView without keyExtractor/recycling for image-heavy detail
mobile_reasoning: Parallax done badly is the canonical example of JS-thread scroll lag. The fix is useAnimatedScrollHandler from Reanimated — it runs ON the UI thread, writes to shared values, and lets you derive everything else (image scale, header opacity) via worklets that never cross the bridge. The sticky title visibility is derived from scrollY > headerThreshold — a worklet decision that updates a shared value, not React state. The native header receives the sticky title via Animated.View opacity at the navigation level.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: ProductDetail screen has Hero, Body, StickyHeader components. All animation driven by single scrollY shared value.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-021, define named constants for useNativeDriver-equivalent: pure-worklet derived values to avoid bridge: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-021, isolate cross_platform differences for useNativeDriver-equivalent: pure-worklet derived values to avoid bridge: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-021, test useNativeDriver-equivalent: pure-worklet derived values to avoid bridge at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: scrollY = useSharedValue from useAnimatedScrollHandler. Hero scale = interpolate(scrollY, [-200, 0], [1.5, 1]). Hero parallax = interpolate(scrollY, [0, HERO_HEIGHT], [0, -HERO_HEIGHT * 0.5]). Hero fade = interpolate(scrollY, [HERO_HEIGHT * 0.6, HERO_HEIGHT], [1, 0]). Sticky title opacity = interpolate(scrollY, [STICKY_TRIGGER, STICKY_TRIGGER + 30], [0, 1]).
gesture_strategy: Standard ScrollView gestures. No custom gesture coordination needed.
interaction_details: Scroll up (overscroll): hero scales up to 1.5x for rubber-band feel
Scroll down: hero parallaxes at 0.5x scroll speed AND fades out past 60% of its height
Past 240px scroll: sticky header title fades in with a subtle 8px translate-up
All values interpolated on UI thread — zero React renders during scroll
Header overlay translucent on iOS (no background) — content shows through; Android opaque for legibility
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-021 (useNativeDriver-equivalent: pure-worklet derived values to avoid bridge): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Defines a measurable hot path for useNativeDriver-equivalent: pure-worklet derived values to avoid bridge; compare before/after on the same device fixture and fail CI when render count, memory, or JS frame time regresses.
what_makes_this_native: 1) UI-thread scroll handler is RN's answer to web's passive scroll listeners — but executes worklets natively. 2) iOS overscroll bounce feeding the rubber-band effect is a platform-native input the worklet can read. 3) Translucent header on iOS uses content underneath as the background — feels like Apple's own apps.

title: Hardware back button + iOS edge swipe coordination on a modal stack
design_brief: A multi-step form (4 screens) presented modally with custom back navigation. iOS users expect to swipe from the left edge to go back; Android users expect the hardware/gesture back button. Both must respect dirty-state confirmation: if the user has typed in the current screen, going back must show a confirmation (action sheet on iOS, AlertDialog on Android). Reference: Apple Settings deep flows, Material 3 form flows.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Disabling all back navigation when form is dirty
Showing a JS Alert.alert on both platforms (looks alien on Android Material apps)
Forgetting BackHandler.addEventListener on Android — back button silently exits app
Allowing iOS edge swipe even when dirty (breaks the dirty-state contract)
Rebuilding the modal stack on every step (loses prior screen state)
mobile_reasoning: Expo Router's native stack respects iOS edge-swipe by default via gestureEnabled. To intercept dirty-state, we disable the native gesture (gestureEnabled:false) and provide our own pan-from-left-edge gesture that fires the confirmation. On Android, BackHandler.addEventListener returns true to consume the back event when dirty, then we show our Material dialog. Same dirty-confirmation flow exposed via different surfaces per platform.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: useUnsavedChangesGuard hook hooks into route params + BackHandler + custom gesture. Confirmation UI per platform.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-022, define named constants for Hardware back button + iOS edge swipe coordination on a modal stack: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-022, isolate platform_specific_behavior differences for Hardware back button + iOS edge swipe coordination on a modal stack: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.

animation_strategy: Confirmation sheets use the same patterns as rn-007. Form navigation uses Expo Router native stack — system handles transitions.
gesture_strategy: iOS: Gesture.Pan().activeOffsetX([15, 9999]) starting only when touch begins within 24px of left edge. Otherwise fails (so vertical scroll works). Android: BackHandler subscription.
interaction_details: iOS: native edge swipe works when not dirty; replaced with custom edge gesture that triggers ActionSheet when dirty
Android: hardware back button intercepted via BackHandler when dirty, opens Material dialog
Confirmation: 'Discard' in destructive color (iOS top, Android right-aligned per platform convention)
Either platform: no confirmation if form is clean — just go back
No global modal stack interference
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-022 (Hardware back button + iOS edge swipe coordination on a modal stack): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Branches only at platform-convention boundaries for Hardware back button + iOS edge swipe coordination on a modal stack; system UI handles native animation where possible and tests iOS/Android parity through snapshots and a11y roles.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Respecting BOTH platforms' back conventions in one hook — iOS edge swipe and Android back button. 2) Confirmation UI per platform: action sheet vs Material dialog. 3) gestureEnabled toggle on Expo Router stack means we cooperate with native nav, not fight it.

title: Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback)
design_brief: A settings overlay panel inspired by iOS 17 Control Center and Apple Music Now Playing: a card with a heavy native blur background, subtle vibrancy effect tinting the blur from underlying content, fine 1.5px stroke outline, and content (toggles, sliders, buttons) floating clearly above. On Android, where real-time backdrop blur is expensive on most devices, fall back to a deeply translucent dark surface with a subtle inner glow. Reference: iOS Control Center, Apple Music Now Playing, macOS Spotlight.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: BlurView on Android with intensity > 30 (kills GPU on mid-tier)
Hardcoded background color when iOS has real blur available
Pretending Android blur looks like iOS blur (it doesn't, and shouldn't)
Stacking multiple BlurViews (each is a render pass)
Using the deprecated react-native-blur — use expo-blur
mobile_reasoning: iOS UIVisualEffectView is GPU-accelerated and free at runtime. Android backdrop blur via expo-blur is implemented but expensive — it works on Pixel devices but stutters on $200 phones. The honest answer is: USE native blur on iOS, use a stylized translucent panel on Android. Don't try to make them look identical. Vibrancy (text/icon tinting based on underlying content) is iOS-only via the tint prop. The 1.5px stroke outline gives the glass a 'physical edge' even where the blur is subtle.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: GlassPanel component branches on Platform.OS for the backdrop layer. Inner content layer identical across platforms.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-023, define named constants for Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback): MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-023, isolate platform_specific_behavior differences for Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback): shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-023, test Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback) at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

gesture_strategy: Tap outside dismisses (Pressable scrim). Pan-down on panel header dismisses if velocity > threshold.
interaction_details: Open: scale 0.92→1 spring + fade in 240ms
Pan down: panel translates with finger, drag past 80px or fast flick dismisses
Tap outside: scrim Pressable dismisses with fade
iOS: backdrop blurs underlying content — content tints through the blur subtly
Android: backdrop is opaque-ish dark; users don't expect blur magic on $200 phones
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-023 (Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback)): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps the visual treatment for Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback) token-driven and stable; measure layout passes after theme changes and ensure contrast/blur fallbacks do not trigger extra renders.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Real iOS UIVisualEffectView via expo-blur — visually identical to Control Center. 2) Honest fallback on Android instead of fake blur — respects the platform's strengths. 3) Reduce Transparency awareness — iOS users with the setting on get the accessible variant automatically.

title: Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts
design_brief: A contacts-app-style sectioned list (Apple Contacts, WhatsApp): items grouped alphabetically with sticky headers that scroll up under the next header. A right-edge scroll bar lets the user jump to a section by dragging. Each section header shows the live count of items in that section. Reference: Apple Contacts, WhatsApp Chats, Apple Music Library.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: SectionList (slower than FlashList, no recycling)
Re-computing section data on every render
Header count from data.filter on every render (re-iterates whole array)
Right-edge scroll bar as separate scrollable list (memory waste)
Hardcoded section header height (FlashList tolerates variance but needs estimate)
mobile_reasoning: FlashList supports stickyHeaderIndices natively in v2 — but data must be a flat array with mixed types (header + item). We precompute the sectioned flat array + indices once via useMemo. Section counts are folded into the header data at compute time — no per-render filtering. The right-edge index scrubber is a single absolutely-positioned column that maps drag-y to section index, calls scrollToIndex on FlashList. Sectioning is data structure work, not React work.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Sectionizer utility produces {flat, indices, sectionOffsets}. FlashList consumes flat. IndexScrubber is sibling.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-024, define named constants for Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-024, isolate cross_platform differences for Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-024, test Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: Scrubber drag: useSharedValue tracks finger y, derives section index, calls runOnJS(scrollToIndex). Optional: small bubble showing current letter follows finger.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pan on scrubber. activeOffsetY([-5, 5]) — should activate on small movement.
interaction_details: Scroll up: sticky letter headers slide under next header (FlashList native behavior)
Drag on right-edge scrubber: selection haptic on each new letter crossed, list jumps to that section
Header shows live count per section (folded into data at sectionize time)
Tap contact: navigates to detail
Smooth 60fps scrolling even with 5000+ contacts (FlashList recycling)
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-024 (Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Uses stable keys and virtualized row boundaries for Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts; profile scroll FPS, cell re-render count, and image/text measurement on a production-sized list.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Right-edge index scrubber with haptic per letter — pure mobile pattern from Apple Contacts. 2) FlashList sticky headers behave like native UITableView sticky headers, including the 'slide under' effect. 3) Selection haptic per section gives audible-feeling feedback during fast scrubbing.

title: Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges
design_brief: A full-screen photo viewer (Apple Photos, Instagram zoom): pinch with two fingers to zoom in to 5x max, pan when zoomed in to navigate around the image, double-tap to zoom to 2x at the tap point (or back to 1x if already zoomed), rubber-band when panning past image bounds, snap back to fit on release. Reference: Apple Photos, Instagram photo zoom, Telegram media viewer.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using a library like react-native-image-zoom-viewer (legacy patterns)
Allowing pan when at scale 1 (should pass to parent for next-photo swipe)
Linear zoom with no rubber-band at max
Pan without bounding to image edges
Resetting transform without spring physics
mobile_reasoning: Pinch + Pan composed with Gesture.Simultaneous. Pan is disabled (or rather, fails) when scale === 1 so the parent ViewPager handles next-photo swipe. The transform origin for pinch must be at the focal point of the two fingers (gesture.focalX/Y), otherwise zoom feels off-center. Bounded pan: when zoomed, the allowed translation = (image_w * scale - screen_w) / 2 on each axis. Past those bounds, rubber-band with diminishing return.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PhotoViewer component owns gestures. Image fills container.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-025, define named constants for Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-025, isolate cross_platform differences for Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-025, test Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: scale, translateX, translateY shared values. On end: clamp scale to [1, 5] with spring. Clamp translation to bounds with spring. Double-tap: scale snaps to target with spring + translation adjusts to keep tap point under finger.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pinch + Gesture.Pan composed simultaneously. Pan.enabled() reactive to scale > 1.05. Gesture.Tap.numberOfTaps(2) for double tap.
interaction_details: Pinch: zooms around the focal point of the two fingers
Single tap: passes through to onSingleTap (e.g., toggle controls overlay)
Double tap: selection haptic + zoom to 2x at tap point (or back to 1x)
Pan when zoomed: follows finger, rubber-bands past bounds (resistance 0.4)
Release: settles to clamped scale + position with spring
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-025 (Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps continuous gesture math for Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges in gesture/Reanimated worklets, commits state once at release, and tests vertical-scroll coexistence plus reduced-motion fallback.
what_makes_this_native: 1) True multi-touch pinch around focal point — web touch events can do this but rarely as smoothly. 2) Rubber-band at bounds is the iOS/Android system convention everyone expects. 3) Single tap vs double tap arbitration via requireExternalGestureToFail handles the 250ms window correctly.

title: Live tracking polyline that animates as new GPS points arrive
design_brief: A Strava/Citymapper live tracking screen: as user moves, GPS pings every 2s, polyline extends along the path with a smooth interpolation rather than a hard new vertex. Current position marker pulses. Map auto-follows the user (but a manual pan disables auto-follow until tap on 'recenter' button). Reference: Strava live tracking, Apple Maps navigation, Citymapper Go.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Calling setState on every GPS ping (re-renders whole map)
Hard-jumping polyline endpoints
No animation between GPS samples
Auto-follow that fights user pan
Listening to location with watchPositionAsync at 1Hz without distanceInterval
mobile_reasoning: GPS samples arrive sparse — interpolating linearly between samples over the 2s interval gives a smooth trail. Polyline coordinates are stored in a useRef array; map.setNativeProps updates coordinates without going through React reconciliation. User pan disables auto-follow via onPanDrag listener. Recenter button re-enables and animates back to user.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: LiveTrackMap component owns Location + MapView refs. Recenter FAB anchored bottom-right above safe area.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-026, define named constants for Live tracking polyline that animates as new GPS points arrive: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-026, isolate cross_platform differences for Live tracking polyline that animates as new GPS points arrive: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.

animation_strategy: User marker pulse: useFrameCallback with sine wave on scale 1↔1.2. Polyline extension: useEffect on new sample interpolates 5 intermediate points and updates polyline ref over 2s.
gesture_strategy: Map's native pan triggers onPanDrag → disables auto-follow. Recenter tap re-enables.
interaction_details: GPS update every ~2s with smooth polyline extension
User position marker pulses softly (1 ↔ 1.25 scale, 900ms ease-in-out sine)
Pan map → recenter FAB appears with auto-follow off
Tap recenter → selection haptic + map animates back to user position
Polyline uses rounded caps + joins for organic feel
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-026 (Live tracking polyline that animates as new GPS points arrive): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Bounds marker/region updates for Live tracking polyline that animates as new GPS points arrive; debounce location writes, cluster or window large marker sets, and measure map interaction FPS while selected-place details update.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Native GPS via expo-location with distanceInterval — won't update for tiny noise jitter. 2) Map-native polyline rendering, not view-based — handles thousands of points without slowdown. 3) Pulse-on-position marker is a long-running animation that costs ~zero JS work via Reanimated.

title: Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback
design_brief: A list pull-to-refresh that doesn't use the default RefreshControl. As user pulls, a custom indicator (e.g., growing arc) tracks the pull distance and rotates when ready to release. On release-past-threshold, the indicator becomes a full spinner; on data arrival, it morphs to a checkmark briefly before sliding away. Reference: Things 3 pull-to-refresh, Apple Mail, Twitter (RIP).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Default <RefreshControl/> with no design intent
Indicator only animating ON release (should track during pull)
Hard release threshold with no visual signal
No success state — list just snaps back
Spinner that keeps spinning forever if data never arrives
mobile_reasoning: useAnimatedScrollHandler tracks overscroll (negative contentOffset.y on iOS). Custom indicator opacity/rotation derived from that overscroll value on UI thread. Release detection: onScrollEndDrag or a JS-side useEffect on a 'pullDistance > threshold' state. After release, indicator switches to spinning mode (rotation via withRepeat). On data arrival, runOnUI to morph to check + slide away. Timeout safety: if no data after 12s, show error and reset.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PullToRefresh component wraps a scrollable child. Indicator sits absolutely above the scroll view.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-027, define named constants for Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-027, isolate cross_platform differences for Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-027, test Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: pullProgress derived from scrollY → [0..1]. Indicator: scale + opacity from 0 to 1 as progress grows. Past 1, rotation indicates 'ready'. On refresh start: rotation runs withRepeat. On complete: stop rotation, morph to check, fade out.
gesture_strategy: None custom — uses ScrollView's native bounce.
interaction_details: Pull begins: indicator fades in proportionally to pull distance
Pull crosses threshold: medium haptic, indicator at full scale
Release past threshold: refresh starts, indicator spins continuously
Data arrives: success haptic, brief solid arc, fades out over 280ms
Timeout (12s): error haptic, indicator vanishes — list isn't stuck
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-027 (Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Uses UI-thread shared values and reduced-motion zero-duration paths for Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback; profile frame time during mount/exit and assert no JS callback runs on every animation frame.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Indicator tracks pull distance in real time on UI thread — feels like the indicator IS the gesture. 2) Haptic at threshold crossing tells the user 'release now' without looking. 3) Built-in timeout protects against the dreaded 'forever spinner' that web apps love to leave you with.

title: Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row
design_brief: A Cron/Things-style native-feeling date picker: three vertical wheel columns (day, month, year) that the user spins with touch. Each row passing the center triggers a selection haptic. Snaps to nearest row on release with deceleration. Selected row visually highlighted with surrounding rows dimmed. Reference: iOS native UIPickerView, Cron calendar, Things 3 date picker.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using @react-native-picker/picker (lots of platform quirks)
FlatList with snapToInterval but no haptic per row
Custom scroll that doesn't decelerate naturally
Haptics on every scroll frame (call once per item index change)
Hardcoded item height that breaks on dynamic type
mobile_reasoning: A Skia-free implementation uses Animated.ScrollView with snapToInterval = itemHeight, decelerationRate='fast'. The selected index is derived from scrollY: round(scrollY / itemHeight). useAnimatedReaction watches that derived index and fires haptic + onChange via runOnJS when it changes. The center is a fixed pill overlay. Top/bottom padding = (visibleRows / 2) * itemHeight so the first and last items can scroll to center.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: WheelColumn component (single column). DateWheel composes 3 columns + selection pill.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-028, define named constants for Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-028, isolate cross_platform differences for Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-028, test Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: scrollY → derived index → useAnimatedReaction. Each visible row's opacity derived from |distance to center|.
gesture_strategy: ScrollView native vertical scroll. No custom gesture.
interaction_details: Spin: selection haptic per row passing center
Snap on release via snapToInterval + decelerationRate='fast'
Rows fade and shrink as they move away from center
Selection pill highlights the center row through all columns
No double-haptic on the same index (useAnimatedReaction tracks prev)
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-028 (Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Keeps typing responsive for Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row by validating after input boundaries, avoiding parent list re-renders, and measuring keypress-to-paint plus keyboard open/close on device.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Per-row haptic gives the wheel a 'detent' feel like an actual physical wheel. 2) Native snapToInterval + decelerationRate='fast' produces the iOS-spec wheel deceleration. 3) Opacity falloff for distant rows is the depth cue that makes the wheel feel 3D without 3D.

title: Sparkline list rows that animate on scroll into view
design_brief: A portfolio/watchlist list (Robinhood, Cash App investing): each row contains a small sparkline trend chart on the right. The sparklines animate-in (line draws from left to right over 400ms) the first time they scroll into view — subsequent appearances are instant. Reference: Robinhood watchlist, Apple Stocks, Cash App investing.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Every sparkline re-animating on every render
SVG paths animated with strokeDashoffset on JS thread
Animating ALL sparklines on initial mount (visible ones first, lazy others)
Using ViewportObserver from web (not a thing on RN — use onViewableItemsChanged)
mobile_reasoning: FlashList exposes onViewableItemsChanged. We track a Set<string> of itemIds that have animated. When a new item enters viewable, mark it and let it animate. The sparkline itself is Skia — strokeDashoffset equivalent done by trimming the path. Animation is per-row UI-thread worklet — no shared state across rows.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: WatchlistRow with Sparkline subcomponent. Animated-once Set held in a ref at list level.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-029, define named constants for Sparkline list rows that animate on scroll into view: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-029, isolate cross_platform differences for Sparkline list rows that animate on scroll into view: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.

animation_strategy: progress shared value 0→1 with withTiming(400ms). Drawn path uses trim — Skia Path.getTrimmed(0, progress).
gesture_strategy: Tap or focus a data mark to expose the exact value; the semantic legend mirrors the same interaction for screen readers.
interaction_details: First scroll into view: sparkline draws left to right over 400ms with ease-out
Recycled cells with previously-seen ids: render at progress=1 (no animation)
Up trend green, down trend red — color encoded
Tabular-num font variant keeps numeric columns aligned
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-029 (Sparkline list rows that animate on scroll into view): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Precomputes chart paths for Sparkline list rows that animate on scroll into view, keeps accessibility summaries in React text, and profiles path recalculation against dataset size and orientation changes.
what_makes_this_native: 1) FlashList's viewable items API is RN-specific — no equivalent on the web outside libraries. 2) Skia path trim runs on UI thread — feels like a native draw animation. 3) Animate-once-per-id pattern keeps fast scrolling smooth (scrolling back to a row doesn't trigger another animation).

title: VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions
design_brief: A custom slider control (volume, brightness, settings) with a thicker thumb than the default, value pill that appears above on drag, and stepped increments. Must work seamlessly with VoiceOver — VoiceOver users can swipe up/down on the slider to increment/decrement, double-tap to activate. Reference: iOS Settings sliders, Apple Music volume, Android Material 3 slider.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: @react-native-community/slider with no accessibility customization
Slider that doesn't expose accessibilityValue (VoiceOver reads '50%' default)
Step values that don't snap visually
Value bubble that appears only on drag end (should appear during drag)
Touch target smaller than 44pt
mobile_reasoning: Custom sliders are an accessibility minefield. The pattern is: accessibilityRole='adjustable', accessibilityValue={{min, max, now, text: human-readable}}, onAccessibilityAction handles 'increment'/'decrement'/'activate'. VoiceOver users get the up/down swipe to step through values without any gesture work — these get routed to the same handlers as touch. The visual is irrelevant to VoiceOver; the contract is in those props.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Slider component with track, fill, thumb, and value pill. Theme tokens for thumb size + colors.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: For rn-030, define named constants for VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions: MIN_TOUCH = 44, safe-area edge padding, and the component-specific sizes used by the snippet instead of anonymous inline numbers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: For rn-030, isolate cross_platform differences for VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions: shadow/elevation, keyboard/safe-area behavior, native picker/sheet/map APIs, and permission prompts stay behind a tiny adapter rather than branching through render code.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: For rn-030, test VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions at 320pt width, large Dynamic Type, landscape/tablet split view, and notched safe areas; derive widths from useWindowDimensions or parent layout and preserve 44px touch targets.

animation_strategy: thumbScale: useSharedValue → withSpring on press. valuePill opacity: useSharedValue → withTiming on drag start/end.
gesture_strategy: Pan gesture on the track. activeOffsetX([-3, 3]) so the slider activates with minimal movement. failOffsetY([-10, 10]) so the slider doesn't fight vertical list scroll.
interaction_details: Drag: selection haptic on each step crossed
iOS: thumb scales up to 1.25 on press (Apple HIG)
Android: thumb stays same size (Material 3)
Value pill fades in above thumb during drag, fades out on release
VoiceOver: swipe up/down on slider increments/decrements by step, same haptic
accessibility_considerations: Accessibility contract for rn-030 (VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions): name the primary native control, keep every Pressable/TextInput/gesture target at least 44px or add hitSlop, expose an explicit non-gesture path for any swipe/pan/long-press behavior, and verify VoiceOver/TalkBack announces state changes without raw implementation details.
performance_notes: Treats accessibility state as the primary contract for VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions; verify TalkBack/VoiceOver output, 44px targets, action alternatives, and no extra render churn from announcements.
what_makes_this_native: 1) accessibilityRole='adjustable' makes VoiceOver swipes work without any custom code. 2) Platform-aware thumb scaling honors HIG (iOS) vs Material (Android). 3) Haptic per step makes the slider feel like a click-stop dial.
